name: Psychological
description: Stories emphasizing internal conflict, perception, and mental pressure, often exploring obsession, identity fracture, or unreliable reality.

nodes.label: Psychological Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story where the primary terrain is the mind — internal conflict, contested perception, and mounting mental pressure are the engines of drama, not external action or physical threat alone.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as psychological genre
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Psychological thriller
Psychological drama
Psychological horror
Identity/dissociation story
failure_modes: Claims psychological depth but relies on surface-level shock or spectacle
Internal conflict is named but never dramatized
signals_in_text: Opening establishes the character's inner world as unstable or under pressure
Reader/viewer is drawn into the character's subjective experience from the start

nodes.label: Internal Conflict Is the Primary Engine
nodes.definition: The story's central conflict must originate and be sustained from within the character's psychology — fears, desires, obsessions, traumas, or fractured identity must drive events more than any external antagonist or circumstance.
exit_conditions: Every major plot development can be traced back to the character's internal state
typical_variants: Obsession that distorts judgment
Trauma that reshapes perception
Repressed memory surfacing as crisis
Identity fracture creating conflicting impulses
failure_modes: External antagonist does all the work while the character merely reacts
Internal conflict is resolved or dissolved midway without replacement
signals_in_text: Character's choices are driven by psychological need rather than rational strategy
The same internal pattern recurs in escalating forms
Danger and safety both originate from within the character

nodes.label: Perception and Reality Are Contested or Unstable
nodes.definition: The story must maintain genuine uncertainty about what is real — the character's perception of events, people, or their own identity must be demonstrably unreliable, contested by others, or structurally unstable.
exit_conditions: At least one sustained thread exists where the audience cannot fully trust the character's account of reality
typical_variants: Unreliable narrator whose version of events is contradicted
Gaslighting where external forces deny the character's reality
Dissociation or identity fracture creating competing versions of self
Delusion or hallucination indistinguishable from reality
failure_modes: All events are presented with stable, agreed-upon reality
Unreliability is announced once but never dramatized through scene construction
signals_in_text: Scenes that retroactively recontextualize earlier events
Contradictions between character's account and physical evidence
Other characters dispute the protagonist's perception directly

nodes.label: Mental and Emotional Pressure Must Escalate
nodes.definition: The psychological burden on the character must increase continuously — each act must push the character closer to a breaking point, with mounting obsession, deteriorating grip on reality, or deepening emotional crisis.
exit_conditions: Each narrative beat measurably increases the character's internal strain
typical_variants: Obsession tightening until it consumes everything
Paranoia that grows to encompass more relationships and situations
Dissociation deepening as stress increases
Grief or guilt metastasizing into self-destruction
failure_modes: Psychological pressure plateaus — character copes adequately throughout
Pressure resets after each crisis without accumulation
signals_in_text: Character's coping mechanisms visibly fail in sequence
Sleep, appetite, and routine deteriorate as markers of internal collapse
The same fear or obsession appears in more extreme form each time

nodes.label: Character's Psychology Is the Source of Both Danger and Resolution
nodes.definition: The same psychological force that creates the story's danger must also be the thing through which resolution — whether healing, destruction, or transformation — is reached. The psychology is not the backdrop; it is the plot.
entry_conditions: Internal conflict and contested reality established
exit_conditions: Resolution, whatever its nature, emerges from a psychological shift or confrontation, not external rescue or coincidence
typical_variants: The obsession that destroys also reveals the buried truth
The dissociated identity that endangers also protects
The delusion that isolates also preserves something vital
The trauma that distorts also holds the key to understanding
failure_modes: Danger comes from the character's psychology but is resolved by external intervention
Resolution ignores the psychological source and patches the surface
signals_in_text: Climax requires the character to face their own mind directly
Resolution changes the character's relationship to their own psychology
The antagonist force and the saving force are revealed as the same thing

nodes.label: Psychological Thriller
nodes.definition: Psychological genre fused with thriller mechanics — the mental instability or contested perception creates suspense and danger, with plot complications arising from the character's unreliable judgment or another's deliberate manipulation of their psychology.
entry_conditions: Suspense and danger are generated by psychological instability or manipulation
exit_conditions: Threat to sanity, identity, or perception drives escalating tension toward a crisis point
typical_variants: Gaslighting thriller where an antagonist systematically destroys the protagonist's reality
Paranoid thriller where the character cannot distinguish real threat from delusion
Obsession thriller where fixation leads to criminal or self-destructive action
Unreliable narrator thriller where the audience discovers the truth before or alongside the character
failure_modes: Psychological dimension is dropped in the third act in favor of pure physical confrontation
The 'twist' that recontextualizes perception is not properly prepared
signals_in_text: Protagonist doubts their own perception while also fearing real external threat
Other characters' behavior is readable as both benign and sinister simultaneously
Plot momentum is driven by psychological deterioration

nodes.label: Psychological Drama
nodes.definition: Psychological genre without thriller suspense mechanics — the drama lies in the interior journey itself, with conflict centered on the character's relationship to their own mind, past, and identity rather than on external danger.
entry_conditions: Primary interest is in psychological process rather than suspense or threat
exit_conditions: The character's psychological arc is the story — insight, breakdown, grief, or transformation is the destination
typical_variants: Grief drama where loss restructures the character's entire perception
Trauma drama where the past intrudes on the present
Identity drama where the character dismantles and reconstructs who they are
Relational drama where one character's psychology distorts an entire relationship system
failure_modes: Psychological material is treated as backstory rather than active present-tense drama
Resolution is too clean — psychological drama rarely ends with full recovery
signals_in_text: Scenes of introspection, therapy, or memory carry the same weight as plot events
Progress is measured in psychological rather than external terms
The character may not 'win' in conventional narrative terms

nodes.label: Psychological Horror
nodes.definition: Psychological genre fused with horror — the dread and threat arise from within the character's own mind, from the collapse of the boundary between self and fear, or from the possibility that the character is the source of the horror.
entry_conditions: Horror atmosphere is generated by psychological instability rather than external monsters
exit_conditions: The audience fears the character's mind as much as any external threat
typical_variants: Haunted protagonist where the 'haunting' is internal
Deterioration horror where the character's psychological collapse is the horrifying event
Culpability horror where the character discovers they are responsible for the horror
Perception horror where reality itself becomes the threat
failure_modes: Horror is revealed to be entirely external, canceling the psychological ambiguity
The dread is atmospheric but never rooted in specific psychological logic
signals_in_text: Horror elements cannot be cleanly attributed to external or internal cause
The character's own mind is the most threatening space in the story
Dread accumulates through psychological detail rather than supernatural imagery

nodes.label: Identity and Dissociation Story
nodes.definition: Psychological genre centered specifically on fragmented or multiple identity — the character's sense of a unified self is disrupted, contested, or revealed to be a construction, with the story exploring the gap between performed identity and inner reality.
entry_conditions: Identity fragmentation or dissociation is the central psychological event
exit_conditions: The relationship between the character's different identity states is the engine of plot and meaning
typical_variants: Dissociative identity story where alternate selves have conflicting agendas
Impostor story where the character has built a false identity over genuine trauma
Transformation story where the character's former identity becomes unrecognizable
Mirror story where another character reflects back a hidden or denied aspect of self
failure_modes: Identity fragmentation is treated as spectacle rather than examined psychologically
The 'true self' is presented as stable and recoverable, undermining the genre's core tension
signals_in_text: The character relates differently to their own history across scenes
Other characters perceive the protagonist as radically different people
The question 'who is this person really' remains genuinely open

nodes.label: Subjective Experience Dominates — What the Character Perceives May Not Be Real
nodes.definition: The story's world must be rendered primarily through the character's subjective experience, so that the audience shares the character's perceptual uncertainty. Objective reality is not privileged over the character's interior reality in narrative presentation.
exit_conditions: Scene construction withholds objective confirmation of events, or stages events so both real and imagined interpretations remain viable
typical_variants: Close third-person or first-person narration that cannot be trusted
Scenes that replay with different details suggesting memory distortion
Physical environment that shifts or transforms in ways that may be literal or metaphorical
Other characters whose words and actions are legible as both supportive and threatening
failure_modes: Omniscient narration confirms what is real, eliminating perceptual uncertainty
Other characters definitively validate or invalidate the protagonist's perception too early
signals_in_text: Scene details are slightly inconsistent across the narrative
Physical descriptions carry psychological weight — the room feels smaller, the light feels wrong
The audience is aware of not being given enough information to adjudicate reality

nodes.label: Confined or Claustrophobic Spaces Reflect Internal State
nodes.definition: Physical settings must externalize psychological condition — enclosed, claustrophobic, or inescapable spaces should mirror the character's internal entrapment, with the environment functioning as a spatial metaphor for psychological constraint.
exit_conditions: Setting choices reinforce rather than contrast with the character's psychological state
typical_variants: Literal confinement (hospital, apartment, institution) reflecting psychological entrapment
Recurring spaces that become more oppressive as psychological pressure increases
Labyrinthine settings that mirror confusion and dissociation
Domestic space that turns hostile as the character's inner world destabilizes
failure_modes: Settings are generic and could belong to any genre
Open, expansive settings are used during the story's most claustrophobic psychological moments
signals_in_text: Space is described in terms of how it feels to the character, not just how it appears
Characters cannot easily leave spaces even when physically able to
Environmental details intensify as psychological pressure intensifies

nodes.label: Relationships Are Tools of Manipulation or Mirrors of Psychology
nodes.definition: Other characters in psychological stories do not exist primarily as independent agents — they function as instruments of manipulation applied to the protagonist's psychology, or as mirrors that reflect back distorted or denied aspects of the protagonist's inner life.
exit_conditions: Each significant relationship either exerts psychological pressure on the protagonist or reveals something about the protagonist's psychology that direct narration cannot
typical_variants: The gaslighter who systematically dismantles the protagonist's reality
The double or shadow figure who embodies the protagonist's denied self
The therapist or confessor who draws out dangerous material
The apparent ally whose care may be control
failure_modes: Supporting characters are psychologically independent from the protagonist's inner drama
Relationships are realistic and functional, providing genuine uncomplicated support
signals_in_text: Protagonist interprets others through the lens of their own psychology
Relationships trigger or amplify psychological symptoms
The most important other character is in some way a reflection of the protagonist's inner state

nodes.label: Reality-Questioning Scene
nodes.definition: A scene in which the character's grasp on reality is directly tested — an event occurs that cannot be explained within the character's existing framework, forcing a confrontation with the possibility that their perception, memory, or understanding is wrong.
exit_conditions: Character is left in genuine uncertainty about what is real, with no immediate resolution
typical_variants: Evidence contradicts the character's memory of events
Another character provides an account of reality that cannot be easily dismissed
Physical environment behaves in a way that defies the character's expectation
The character catches themselves doing something they have no memory of
failure_modes: Reality question is immediately resolved — character quickly determines what is real
Scene is purely atmospheric without specific challenged belief or memory
signals_in_text: Character explicitly or implicitly questions their own mind
Scene ends on uncertainty rather than restored stability
The audience is equally uncertain about what has happened

nodes.label: Obsession and Breakdown Escalation Scene
nodes.definition: A scene that dramatizes a significant escalation in the character's obsessive or deteriorating psychological state — the coping mechanisms fail, the obsession breaks containment, or the accumulated pressure produces a visible rupture.
entry_conditions: Psychological pressure has been building across prior scenes
exit_conditions: Character's behavior crosses a threshold — something previously controlled becomes uncontrolled, or a hidden psychological state becomes visible to others
typical_variants: Obsession that previously operated in private erupts into the character's public life
Emotional breakdown that reveals the extent of deterioration to others
Act of violence, self-harm, or destruction driven by psychological pressure
Dissociative episode that creates consequences the character must account for
failure_modes: Escalation is described but not dramatized in scene
Breakdown occurs too early before sufficient pressure has been built
Character recovers too quickly, resetting the psychological pressure
signals_in_text: Scene is more intense than anything that has preceded it
Character's behavior is genuinely alarming to witnesses
The audience recognizes this as a point of no return

nodes.label: Revelation or Confrontation-with-Self Scene
nodes.definition: The climactic psychological scene in which the character must face the truth about their own mind — whether that is a repressed memory, a denied action, the true nature of their obsession, or the reality of their own fractured identity.
entry_conditions: All psychological escalation has led to this point
The character can no longer avoid the interior confrontation
exit_conditions: The character's relationship to their own psychology is transformed — they cannot return to the prior state of denial, delusion, or avoidance
typical_variants: Memory recovered or reconstructed that recontextualizes the entire story
Character confronts their own reflection — literal or metaphorical — and cannot deny what they see
The 'other' the character has been fighting is revealed as a part of themselves
Character must choose between the comfort of delusion and the danger of truth
failure_modes: Revelation is delivered as exposition rather than experienced in scene
Character accepts the revelation without meaningful psychological cost
Confrontation is resolved externally rather than through the character's own psychology
signals_in_text: Scene has the quality of something long-avoided finally arriving
Character's prior understanding of themselves is visibly destroyed
Resolution — whatever its nature — is internal before it is external

nodes.label: Tone of Unsettling Interiority and Mounting Claustrophobia
nodes.definition: Psychological genre requires a pervasive tone that privileges interior experience — the reader or viewer is inside the character's consciousness even when it is unsafe there, and the cumulative effect is claustrophobic as the narrative space shrinks to the dimensions of the character's mind.
exit_conditions: Tone maintains the texture of close, uncomfortable interiority throughout, never offering the relief of a detached external perspective
typical_variants: Obsessive close narration that circles and returns to the same fixations
Unreliable voice that the audience gradually learns to read against
Atmosphere of creeping wrongness without identifiable external cause
Dread that is quiet and accumulative rather than sudden
failure_modes: Tone steps back into comfortable external perspective during crucial psychological scenes
Interiority is provided in summary rather than dramatized moment-to-moment
signals_in_text: Prose or scene construction stays uncomfortably close to the character's perception
Quiet scenes feel more threatening than conventionally dramatic ones
The reader or viewer experiences something analogous to the character's psychological state

nodes.label: Surface-Level Craziness Without Psychological Logic
nodes.definition: Psychological genre fails when 'psychological' is treated as a synonym for erratic, unpredictable, or shocking behavior without genuine psychological logic — when the character's instability is spectacle rather than a coherent interior process with roots in specific psychology.
exit_conditions: Instability is grounded in specific, consistent psychological logic traceable to character history, wound, or structure
typical_variants: Character behaves bizarrely for atmosphere without consistent underlying psychology
Twist relying on mental illness as pure plot device
Breakdown presented as scary performance rather than painful process
Psychiatric diagnosis used as shorthand for character depth
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience cannot identify the psychological logic beneath the behavior
Mental distress is aestheticized without being understood
The character's instability serves the plot's needs rather than emerging from their own interior life

edges.label: Promise requires internal conflict as primary engine
edges.effects_on_stakes: The character's inner life is the source of all danger and consequence
edges.effects_on_character: Character cannot escape the central conflict by changing circumstances — it lives inside them
anti_patterns: External antagonist drives the plot while inner life remains stable

edges.label: Promise requires contested or unstable perception
edges.effects_on_stakes: The audience cannot be certain what is happening, which implicates them in the character's uncertainty
edges.effects_on_character: Character must navigate without a reliable account of their own experience
anti_patterns: Stable objective reality confirmed throughout — character may be troubled but perceives accurately

edges.label: Promise requires escalating psychological pressure
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each act must be psychologically more costly than the last
edges.effects_on_character: Character's coping mechanisms are systematically dismantled
anti_patterns: Pressure is consistent throughout with no escalation toward breakdown

edges.label: Promise requires psychology as source of both danger and resolution
edges.effects_on_stakes: Resolution cannot come from outside — only from within the character's psychology
edges.effects_on_character: Character must confront themselves to resolve the story
anti_patterns: Danger from psychology resolved by external rescue or circumstance

edges.label: Promise requires unsettling interiority and claustrophobic tone
edges.effects_on_stakes: The narrative space itself becomes claustrophobic as pressure builds
edges.effects_on_character: Character exists in a world rendered entirely through their compromised perception
anti_patterns: Comfortable external perspective offered as relief from interiority

edges.label: Contested reality generating suspense and danger
preconditions: Reality instability creates suspense mechanics and credible threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist may be in real danger they cannot accurately perceive, or perceive danger that is not real
edges.effects_on_character: Character must make survival decisions without reliable perception
anti_patterns: Perceptual instability without any accompanying threat or danger

edges.label: Internal conflict as primary dramatic subject without thriller mechanics
preconditions: Internal conflict is the story's entire subject — no external danger required
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are psychological: insight, breakdown, transformation, or loss of self
edges.effects_on_character: Character's arc is measured in psychological terms rather than external achievement
anti_patterns: Internal conflict treated as backstory while external plot drives the narrative

edges.label: Escalating pressure generating horror atmosphere from within
preconditions: Psychological pressure escalates to the point where the character's own mind becomes the source of horror
edges.effects_on_stakes: The interior world is more threatening than any external danger
edges.effects_on_character: Character cannot escape the horror because it originates in themselves
anti_patterns: Horror is clearly external — psychological framing is incidental

edges.label: Psychology as source specifically through fragmented or multiple identity
preconditions: The source of both danger and resolution is a fractured or multiple identity structure
edges.effects_on_stakes: The character's sense of unified self is at stake
edges.effects_on_character: Resolution requires integration, acceptance, or transformation of fragmented identity
anti_patterns: Identity fragmentation as plot twist without genuine psychological exploration

edges.label: Psychological thriller requires subjective perceptual dominance
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience shares the character's inability to trust their own perception
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot get outside their own compromised point of view
anti_patterns: Objective confirmation of reality available through secondary characters or narration

edges.label: Psychological drama externalizes interiority through confined space
edges.effects_on_stakes: The physical environment mirrors and amplifies the character's psychological constriction
edges.effects_on_character: Character cannot escape the setting any more than they can escape their own psychology
anti_patterns: Open, expansive settings used throughout psychological drama without ironic function

edges.label: Psychological horror routes the horror through relational mirrors
edges.effects_on_stakes: Other characters become surfaces reflecting the protagonist's feared self back at them
edges.effects_on_character: The protagonist cannot engage with others without confronting their own horror
anti_patterns: Horror is entirely atmospheric — no relational channel for the psychological content

edges.label: Identity/dissociation story requires subjective dominance to sustain ambiguity
edges.effects_on_stakes: The audience cannot determine which identity state represents the 'true' protagonist
edges.effects_on_character: Each identity state has a consistent but incompatible subjective reality
anti_patterns: One identity state is definitively privileged as real by narrative structure

edges.label: Subjective dominance makes reality-questioning scene possible
preconditions: Subjective perceptual dominance is established as the narrative mode
edges.effects_on_stakes: The scene crystallizes what has been diffuse — perceptual instability becomes acute crisis
edges.effects_on_character: Character cannot continue without acknowledging the fracture in their reality
anti_patterns: Reality question is immediately resolved — perceptual crisis is short-circuited

edges.label: Confinement accelerates obsession toward visible breakdown
preconditions: Claustrophobic setting has been sustaining and amplifying psychological pressure
edges.effects_on_stakes: The confined setting provides no relief valve — pressure must find another outlet
edges.effects_on_character: Character's containment of the obsession fails when spatial containment is also total
common_alternatives: Institutional setting where breakdown is observed by authorities
anti_patterns: Character escapes the confined space before breakdown — pressure dissipates externally

edges.label: Relational mirror forces confrontation-with-self
preconditions: The most significant relationship has been functioning as a mirror for the protagonist's denied psychology
edges.effects_on_stakes: The relationship must break, transform, or be revealed as illusory for the confrontation to occur
edges.effects_on_character: Confrontation with the other is simultaneously confrontation with the self they have been denying
common_alternatives: Double recognized and integrated or rejected
anti_patterns: Revelation occurs in isolation from the relational structure that enabled the denial

edges.label: Reality-questioning destabilizes defenses, accelerating breakdown
preconditions: Reality-questioning scene has undermined the character's coping structure
edges.effects_on_stakes: Loss of reality grip removes the last defense against the full force of the obsession or pressure
edges.effects_on_character: Without stable reality, the character cannot contain or redirect the psychological pressure
common_alternatives: Brief stabilization followed by more severe collapse
anti_patterns: Reality questioning has no psychological consequence — character stabilizes and continues as before

edges.label: Breakdown clears the ground for confrontation-with-self
preconditions: Breakdown has destroyed the character's prior psychological structure
edges.effects_on_stakes: After breakdown, the character has nothing left to protect — only the confrontation with the truth remains
edges.effects_on_character: The character who enters the confrontation scene is not the same as the one who began the story
common_alternatives: Brief moment of clarity between breakdown and confrontation
anti_patterns: Breakdown is resolved without producing the confrontation — character rebuilds prior defenses

edges.label: Internal conflict without psychological logic produces surface craziness
preconditions: Psychological instability is depicted without traceable interior logic
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience cannot engage with the character's psychology — it becomes spectacle
edges.effects_on_character: Character becomes a vehicle for plot rather than the source of meaning
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
